title: ‘Drive-Away Dolls’ and Book Club No. 1—Francis Ford Coppola, ‘Apocalypse Now,’ and ‘The Path to Paradise’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins review Ethan Coen and Tricia Cooke's 'Drive-Away Dolls' with critic Adam Nayman, then open the first Big Picture Book Club on Sam Wasson's 'The Path to Paradise,' a portrait of Francis Ford Coppola built around the making of 'Apocalypse Now' and the collapse of American Zoetrope.

films.title: Drive-Away Dolls
films.note: Ethan Coen's solo narrative feature, co-written with his wife Tricia Cooke and directed without Joel. A 1999-set comic caper with Margaret Qualley and Geraldine Viswanathan who pick up the wrong drive-away car and stumble into a criminal errand. Nayman recommends it and frames it as a personal, out-and-vocal Cooke project; the group calls it a casual 84-minute romp that plays like half a Coen movie but prefers its looseness to the polish of Joel's 'The Tragedy of Macbeth.' Qualley's Southern accent divided them; the surprise supporting cast (Pedro Pascal, Colman Domingo, Bill Camp, Beanie Feldstein, Miley Cyrus, plus an unnamed movie-star cameo) got singled out. Sean's wife Eileen loved it.

segments.heading: Big Picture Book Club No. 1: ‘The Path to Paradise’
segments.note: Sam Wasson on Francis Ford Coppola

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.note: The spine of Wasson's book, which uses the torturous Philippines shoot as a metaphor for how Coppola chased everything. Sean and Amanda both love the film but feel its making is over-covered ground; Amanda points to Eleanor Coppola's documentary 'Hearts of Darkness' and her book 'Notes on the Making of Apocalypse Now.' The telexes from set (cases of champagne, witch hazel, every Bob Dylan record, a paperback 'Shogun') are their favorite primary sourcing.

films.title: One from the Heart
films.note: The book's other center: the soundstage Las Vegas jukebox musical, scored by Tom Waits, that bombed and sank American Zoetrope. Sean saw the reissued, roughly 15-minutes-shorter 'Reprise' cut and praises Raul Julia and Teri Garr. Read here as tangled up in the Coppolas' marriage.

films.title: The Godfather
films.note: Wasson is sharp on why Coppola unlocked it, seeing himself as Michael and his brother August as Sonny, but the making gets only a couple thirds of a chapter, which suited Sean fine.

films.title: The Godfather Part II
films.note: Sean calls it possibly the greatest American film ever made and wishes the book spent more time on it.

films.title: The Conversation
films.note: Sean wanted an entire chapter; the surveillance thriller reflects the gadget-kid Coppola that Wasson describes. Barely covered.

films.title: Megalopolis
films.note: The self-financed passion project (reportedly $100M of Coppola's own money, built in Peachtree City, Georgia) the book quietly builds toward and ends on as a cliffhanger.

films.title: The Rain People
films.note: The transitional early feature, starring Shirley Knight, that American Zoetrope produced before 'The Godfather.'

films.title: Rumble Fish
films.note: Amanda's favorite post-'Apocalypse Now' Coppola: the stylized black-and-white Hinton adaptation. Gets about one paragraph in the book.

films.title: The Outsiders
films.note: Sean's soft spot from a young age; the other Hinton adaptation that introduced a wave of young actors.

films.title: Gardens of Stone
films.note: Sean's other pick: Coppola's least-seen movie, with James Caan in a quiet, complicated performance.

films.title: The Godfather Part III
films.note: Sean is mildly for it; they relitigate blaming Coppola, not just Sofia, for her casting and wonder about the Winona Ryder version.

films.title: Bram Stoker's Dracula
films.note: The paycheck movie Sean says Coppola 'absolutely crushed' into a hit. Per lore, it's also where Sofia met Keanu Reeves.

films.title: The Rainmaker
films.note: Amanda's stated favorite; Sean owns a region-free player mostly to play the Japanese import Blu-ray.

films.title: Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse
films.note: Eleanor Coppola's making-of documentary, drawn from her on-set notes; Amanda's reference point for how much of this story is already told.

films: Jerry Lee Lewis: Trouble in Mind
